coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of Au@N-CQDs@Pd core-shell nanoparticles for enhanced methanol electrooxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Cong
Yang, Jianghua
Li, Jinling
He, Shijie
Meng, Bowen
Shao, Tao
Zhang, Qiankun
Zhang, Dongxia
Zhou, Xibin
coredata.dc:description: A strategy for the synthesis of Au@N-CQDs@Pd three-layer core-shell nanoparticles without the use of any surfactant is proposed in this work. Herein, N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) are used as the reducing agent, stabilizing agent, and shape-directing agent. The morphology and structure of the final product were characterized by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), and X-ray powder diffraction (XRD). The electrocatalytic test indicates the as-synthesized Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20 exhibites excellent activity for methanol oxidation in alkaline media, which is approximately 6 times higher than commercial Pd/C. And its resistance to CO and stability are also greatly improved. This work provides a simple and straightforward approach to prepare bimetallic core-shell electrocatalyst by using CQDs with high catalytic activity for alkaline fuel cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon quantum dots
Au-Pd nanoparticles
Core-shell structure
Methanol oxidation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of N-CQDs. (B) TEM image of Au@N-CQDs. Insets show the corresponding size distribution in image (A) and (B)·(C)HRTEM images of Au@N-CQDs.(D)HAADF-STEM image and cross-sectional compositional line profiles of a single Au@N-CQDs nanoparticle.

(A) Low, (B) medium, and (C-D) magnification TEM images of Au@N-CQDs@Pd. (E) HAADF-STEM image and cross-sectional compositional line profiles of a single Au@N-CQDs@Pd nanoparticle. (F) EDX spectrum of Au@N-CQDs@Pd.
XRD patterns of N-CQDs, Au@N-CQDs and Au@N-CQDs@Pd.
UV–vis absorption spectra of Au@N-CQDs and Au@N-CQDs@Pd.
TEM images of (A) Au@N-CQDs, (B) Au@N-CQDs@Pd 10, (C) Au@N-CQDs@Pd 15 (D) Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20, (E) Au@N-CQDs@Pd 25 and (F) Au@N-CQDs@Pd 30.
(A) CVs of different catalysts modified electrodes in 0.5 M H2SO4. (B) CVs of different catalysts modified electrodes in 1.0 M KOH containing 0.5 M CH3OH. Scan rate: 50 mV s−1.

(A) CVs of Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20, AuPd and Pd/C catalysts modified electrodes in 0.5 M H2SO4. (B) CVs of Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20, AuPd and Pd/C catalysts modified electrodes in 1 M KOH containing 0.5 M CH3OH. Scan rate: 50 mV s−1 .

(A) CVs of Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20 in 1.0 M KOH at 50 mV s−1 with for various concentrations of methanol: 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 3.5 and 4 M. (B) Plot of the peak current density vs. methanol concentration.

(A) Anodic CV curves of Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20 in 1.0 M KOH containing 0.5 M CH3OH at different scan rates of 10 mV s−1, 30 mV s−1, 50 mV s−1, 70 mV s−1, 90 mV s−1, 100 mV s−1,150 mV s−1, and 200 mV s−1. (B) Plot of peak current density vs. square root of scan rate.
Nyquist plots of GCE, Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20, AuPd and Pd/C catalysts in a solution containing 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4- and 0.1 M KCl.
CA curves of Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20, AuPd and Pd/C catalysts measured in 1.0 M KOH containing 0.5 M CH3OH at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1.
Illustration of the Au@N-CQDs@Pd preparation process and its application for methanol electrooxidation.
Electrochemical performance for the oxidation of methanol.

row.entry:
1 M KOH + 0.5 M CH3OH
0.5 M KOH + 1 M CH3OH

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of Au@N-CQDs@Pd core-shell nanoparticles for enhanced methanol electrooxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Geography and Environment Science, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province
College of Geography and Environment Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Geography and Environment Science, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A strategy for the synthesis of Au@N-CQDs@Pd three-layer core-shell nanoparticles without the use of any surfactant is proposed in this work. Herein, N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) are used as the reducing agent, stabilizing agent, and shape-directing agent. The morphology and structure of the final product were characterized by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), and X-ray powder diffraction (XRD). The electrocatalytic test indicates the as-synthesized Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20 exhibites excellent activity for methanol oxidation in alkaline media, which is approximately 6 times higher than commercial Pd/C. And its resistance to CO and stability are also greatly improved. This work provides a simple and straightforward approach to prepare bimetallic core-shell electrocatalyst by using CQDs with high catalytic activity for alkaline fuel cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Considering the increasingly acute contradiction between traditional fossil fuels and environmental protection, the research and development of clean and efficient new energy sources is imminent. Direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) have received extensive attention with many distinguished advantages such as high energy density, simple operation, low handing temperature and carrying expedience. Until now, DMFCs have emerged as a promising candidate for future energy storage and conversion devices [1–4]. However, a pivotal step in the commercialization of DMFCs is to develop low-cost, high-efficiency anode catalysts [5].

As we all know, Pt and Pt-based catalysts are the most commonly used as electrocatalysts in DMFCs [6,7]. Nevertheless, the high cost, poor durability and scarcity of Pt have severely conditioned its practical utilization [8]. Furthermore, Pt-based catalysts are susceptible to poisoning by intermediate products (such as CO) produced by methanol oxidatin to reduce their electrocatalytic activity and even shorten the useful lifespan to some extent [9]. Comparatively, further studies to replace or reduce Pt-based catalysts are under continuous research are still needed. Interest in Pd-based nanostructures for methanol oxidation have been rapidly growing [10,11]. More importantly, its relatively inexpensive cost and abundant reserves make Pd a good option. Specially, AuPd bimetallic nanoparticles with core-shell [12] or alloy [13] structures are an attractive target of research filed due to their enhanced properties, such as surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [14,15], electrocatalysis [16–19] and electrochemical sensor [20].

Significantly, bimetallic core-shell nanoparticles can minimize the use of precious metal precursors and exhibit enhanced catalytic activity for the unique structure [21]. Up to now, tremendous efforts have been dedicated to the preparation of Au@Pd core-shell nanoparticles of various shapes. To assuage the agglomeration of nanoparticles during preparation process, it depends on the use of different stabilizers to coat the metal nanoparticles so that they can stably exist in the system. For example, Wang's group spent two days synthesizing Au@Pd core-shell nanoparticles with different morphologies by changing the molar ratios of CTAB to CTAC as stabilizers [22]. Lee's group obtained Au@Pd core-shell nanooctahedron at 90 °C by employing CTAC as the surfactant [23]. Unfortunately, the presence of these coating agents not only increases the steric hindrance of the metal particles surface, but also makes it difficult for the reaction substrate to approach the surface of the catalyst, thus resulting in a decrease of catalytic activity [24]. Meanwhile, their large-scale commercialization is greatly limited by harsh reaction conditions. Thus, it is highly desirable to explore a facile approach to produce nanocatalysts with high surface areas in the absence of surfactant.

It has been reported that carbon materials such as graphene [25] and carbon nanotubes [26] can be used to support metal nanoparticles to reduce agglomeration. Particularly, Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a novel type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with a size smaller than 10 nm, which shows a promising candidate for many application, such as fluorescence sensing [27], biomedical imaging [28], light-emitting diodes [29] and catalysis [30,31] owing to its powerful adjustable photoluminescence capability, low biological toxicity and rapid electron transfer speed [32,33]. The hydroxyl and carboxy groups on the surface of CQDs function as both reducer and nucleation supported to benefit growth of metallic nanoparticles [34,35]. For instance, Sha's group loaded Pt on CQDs surface by electrodeposition to form nanoflower structure [36].

Currently, the research on doping of CQDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen has drawn a lot of interest, aiming to improving performance of CQDs, especially in the field of electrocatalysis [37]. Doping with N-CQDs can significantly show a good electrocatalytic activity, which are considered as sites for promoting catalytic to have a large number of defects on the surface and edges [38]. As mentioned above, we synthesized of Au@N-CQDs@Pd core-shell electrocatalyst without any traditional stabilizer and surfactant for the methanol oxidation reaction (MOR). Herein, N-CQDs serve as the reducing agent, stabilizing agent, and shape-directing agent. It is found that the as-prepared Au@N-CQDs@Pd nanoparticles are uniformly dispersed and exhibit the excellent catalytic performance toward MOR compared with AuPd nanoparticles without N-CQDs and commercial Pd/C. The preparation process is depicted in Scheme 1 .

Gold chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O) and commercial Pd/C were bought from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Palladium chloride (PdCl2) was obtained from Keda Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shenyang, China). Sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O), ammonium bicarbonate (NH4HCO3) was purchased from Kaixin Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Ascorbic acid (AA) was obtained from Zhongqin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used as received without further purification. Twice-distilled water was used through the all experimental processes. A 10 mM H2PdCl4 aqueous solution was prepared by completely dissolving 44.5 mg PdCl2 in 25 mL of 20 mM HCl in a 60 °C water bath.

N-CQDs were prepared by the hydrothermal method as described in previous report [39]. Briefly, Na3C6H5O7·2H2O (0.6 g) and NH4HCO3 (4.5 g) were dissolved in 30 mL water under vigorous stirring for 30 min, then placed in a drying oven at 180 °C for 4 h. After cooling down, the product solution was further treated with dialysis bag (molecular weight of 8000-14,000 Da) for three days (replaced twice-distilled water every 12 h). N-CQDs powder was weighted after drying and added to twice-distilled water to form a solution of 400 mg L−1.

According to the literature, N-CQDs were used as reducing agent to directly reduce HAuCl4 to prepare Au@N-CQDs [40]. HAuCl4·3H2O solution (245 μL, 25.4 mM) was added to the N-CQDs (10 mL, 0.08 mg mL−1) and kept at 100 °C for 80 min in an oil bath under light conditions. The solution changed from yellow to purplish red, showing the formation of Au@N-CQDs.

Subsequently, H2PdCl4 (300 μL, 10 mM) and AA (240 μL, 0.4 mM) were successively added to Au@N-CQDs solution with stirring for an hour at 20 °C. The color of the mixture solution changed from the initial purple to the final dark brown and finally collected by centrifugation, washed with ethanol and water (1:1) several times.

The morphology and structures were studied on a FEI TECNAI20 electron microscope (TEM, U.S.) operating at at 80 kV. Element detection was analyzed on a FEI TECNAI20 electron microscope equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy detector (EDX).The crystal structures were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku D/max-2400, Cu Kα1 0.154056 nm radiation).UV–vis absorption spectra was recorded on an Agilent UV-8453 spectrophotometer (Agilent Inc., Jpn).

A CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Co., China) using a conventional three-electrode system was employed for electrochemical measurements at ambient temperature. The working electrode was modified by dropping the 5 μL material onto the glass carbon electrode (GCE, diameter: 3 mm, geometric area: 0.071 cm2). Pt wire electrode and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were used as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. For the catalytic oxidation of methanol, cyclic voltametry (CV) was measured in 1 M KOH containing 0.5 M CH3OH. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed in 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4- (1:1) mixture containing 0.1 M KCl and the alternating current voltage was 5 mV. The electrolyte solutions were purged with high-purity N2 before use for about 30 min.

As shown by the TEM (Fig. 1A), N-CQDs are uniformly dispersed in a small size of 0.53–4.77 nm, with an average diameter of 2.1 ± 0.4 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. S1) displays two strong peaks at 235 and 340 nm attributing to the aromatic sp2 domains with π–π⁎ transition and the excited state energy captured by the surface states [41,42]. In addition, the inset pictures of N-CQDs display bright yellow when the reaction is done (left) and bright blue under light with an emission wavelength of 365 nm (right).

Au@N-CQDs are regular spherical grains with good dispersion, while the particle size is homogeneous with the average diameter of 13 ± 2 nm (Fig. 1B). The outermost layer of each Au@N-CQDs nanoparticle has a relatively bright halo, which is related to the N-CQDs layer wrapped around the darker Au core, demonstrating the formation of Au@N-CQDs. The HRTEM image of several Au@N-CQDs (Fig. 1C) clearly reveals that the Au cores are coated by an ultrathin shell about 1.8 nm thicknesses. This value is basically consistent with the average diameter of the N-CQDs. The compositional line profiles for a single particle also reflect that the core-shell structure of the Au@N-CQDs (Fig. 1D).

Next, the representative TEM images of the Au@N-CQDs@Pd nanoparticles are shown in Fig. 2 . The smooth spherical Au@N-CQDs nanoparticles are covered with multi-branched nanoparticles after reducing the Pd precursor with AA for 1 h, (Fig. 2A and B), forming core-shell dendrites Au@N-CQDs@Pd nanoparticles. The average size is measured to be 17.6 ± 2.2 nm (Fig. 2A), which increases compared with the particle size of Au@N-CQDs. Au@N-CQDs@Pd core-shell nanodendrites can be clearly observed in HRTEM image (Fig. 2C and D). It is beneficial to increase electrocatalytic active site and thus improving catalytic property for oxidation of methanol. The lattice spacing indicated by the core part is 0.235 nm, which corresponds to the lattice constant of the face-centered cubic Au on plane (111) [43], while the lattice spacing indicated by the shell part is 0.225 nm, which is the lattice constant of the typical face-centered cubic Pd on plane (111) [44]. Moreover, the high-angle annular dark-field scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM) image and the elemental line scanning analysis confirm that the core-shell nanostructure is composed of the Au as a core and Pd as a shell (Fig. 2E). The corresponding EDS analysis proves the coexistence of Au, N-CQDs and Pd elements (Fig. 2F). These results are strongly verified the formation of Au@N-CQDs@Pd core-shell nanoparticles with obvious light and dark differences.

To check crystalline phase structure, the as-prepared N-CQDs, Au@N-CQDs and Au@N-CQDs@Pd were characterized using XRD analysis. As can be seen in Fig. 3 , the presence of N-CQDs is confirmed by a wider diffraction peak at 2θ of 26°, which is a typical C (002) diffraction peak of CQDs [43]. The Pd diffraction peaks at 40.1°, 46.5° and 68°correspond to (111) (200) and (220) planes in the face-centered cubic (fcc) structure. Meanwhile, the strong Au diffraction peaks at 38.3°, 44.7°and 64.6° corresponding to (111) (200) and (220) planes reveal that the nanocomposite is a core-shell catalyst consisting of distinct Au and Pd crystalline components rather than an alloy phase, which can be matched with the lattice planes of Au (JCPDS NO. 04–0784) and Pd (JCPDS NO. 46–1043), respectively [21,45].

Simultaneously, the formation of Au@N-CQDs and Au@N-CQDs@Pd were analyzed by UV–vis spectra. As shown in Fig. 4 , Au@N-CQDs show a typical plasmon absorption peak at 543 nm related to Au cores as previously reported (the green line marks this peak) [40]. For the Au@N-CQDs@Pd core-shell nanocomposites, owing to no characteristic peak for the case of Pd nanoparticles, the absorption peak at approximately 543 nm disappeared, which proves the formation of Pd shell with reduction of the Pd salts to the surface of Au@N-CQDs [46].

Remarkably, the raw materials for synthesis of Au@N-CQDs@Pd core-shell catalyst are straightforward. N-CQDs are used in the reduction of HAuCl4 as well as the formation of Pd shell on the outer layer of Au@N-CQDs. In order to better understand the growth process of Au@N-CQDs@Pd, the effects of morphology on the electrochemical performance toward methanol oxidation were systematically studied by changing the reaction temperature in the preparation process. A series of Au@N-CQDs@Pd catalysts were prepared, named Au@N-CQDs@Pd 10, Au@N-CQDs@Pd 15, Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20, Au@N-CQDs@Pd 25 and Au@N-CQDs@Pd 30 when the temperature was adjusted to 10 °C, 15 °C, 20 °C, 25 °C and 30 °C, respectively.

Au@N-CQDs show well-defined spherical shape and fair uniformity (Fig. 5A). Increasing the reaction temperature during the reduction of Pd precursor results in growth of particles, the surface coverage of nanoparticles also increases. For those Au@N-CQDs@Pd nanoparticles made at 10 °C (Fig. 5B), the palladium particles on Au@N-CQDs surfaces are small and few. Well-defined and uniform-size Au@N-CQDs@Pd nanoparticles with a dendritic shell were formed at 20 °C (Fig. 5D). The palladium particles on Au@N-CQDs surface tend to grow much larger while the temperature reaches 25 °C (Fig. 5E). This is due to the occurrence of Ostwald ripening when the reaction temperature exceeds 20 °C in the system, leading to a decrease in the number of Pd particles and an increase in particle size distribution [24]. And yet, when the reaction temperature is raised to 30 °C, it is undefined and irregular nanoparticles that are generated, and a large area of agglomeration is occurred (Fig. 5F). The higher temperature results in very low dispersity.

Our efforts exhibit that the nanoparticles morphology can be effectively controlled by simply altering the temperature during the preparation process. By a series of controlled experiments related temperature, 20 °C was selected as the appropriate reduction temperature. In the whole process of Au@N-CQDs@Pd preparation, N-CQDs amazing act as the reducing agent, stabilizing agent and shape-directing agent. Objective to further insight into the catalytic performance of Au@N-CQDs@Pd in methanol oxidation, we prepare AuPd nanoparticles without N-CQDs in the same way and under the same conditions for comparison. HAuCl4 was reduced with sodium citrate instead of N-CQDs. It is clearly to observe that the AuPd nanoparticles have no obvious dendritic core-shell structure (Fig. S2), while has a cladding structure and aggregates severely, which greatly leads to decreasing the number of active sites.

As a crucial parameter of electrocatalytic activity, electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) is a significant indicator of the number of electrochemical active sites [47]. According to the literature [48], the ECSA of the Au@N-CQDs@Pd is calculated from the integration of reductive peak area marked by the dotted box in Fig. 6A, with 424 μC cm−2 is the palladiums conversion factor. Furthermore, a high special surface area of 90.54 m2 g−1 was confirmed for the Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20 core-shell nanodendrites, higher than that of Au@N-CQDs@Pd 10 approximately 6 times (15.08 m2 g−1). Meanwhile, the CV curves were also recorded in 1.0 M KOH solution to calculate ECSA by the same method (Fig. S3) [13]. Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20 exhibited an outstandingly high ECSA of 142.32 m2 g−1 among the nanocomposites prepared at different temperatures, which was much higher than that of AuPd (13.52 m2 g−1) and Pd/C (4.69 m2 g−1). The largely high ECSA was likely due to the dendritic morphology forms with Pd nanoparticles evenly dispersed on the surface of Au@N-CQDs and lead to more accessible surface active sites, which can also benefit the electrocatalytic processes [19,49].

For further investigating the electrocatalytic activity, the CV tests were measured in N2-saturated 1 M KOH containing 0.5 M CH3OH, as shown in Fig. 6B. Two diverse peaks in the forward and backward scanning corresponded to methanol oxidation and the removal of incompletely oxidized carbonaceous substances [50]. In addition, Au is an oxophilic metal that can oxidize the intermediate product adsorbed on the Pd [51]. The COads stems from the methanol dehydrogenation, which can be further oxidized as follows here [11]:
CO ads
— Pd — Au —
OH ads
→ Pd — CO — Au — OH
→ Pd — Au — CO — OH
(1)
→ Pd — Au — COOH
(2)
Pd — Au — COOH +
OH ads
→ Pd — Au +
CO 2
+
H 2
O

As previously reported, the oxidation kinetic of methanol in alkaline media is superior to that in acidic media. This may be due to the less corrosive nature of the alkaline solution, which reduces cross-over of alcohol, thus reducing catalyst poisoning [52]. The forward peak current densities of Au@N-CQDs@Pd 10, Au@N-CQDs@Pd 15, Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20, Au@N-CQDs@Pd 25 and Au@N-CQDs@Pd 30 were 10.62, 17.88, 25.08, 23.22 and 14.07 mA cm−2, respectively. The ratio of forward/backward peak values (If/Ib) in CV curves of methanol oxidation process can indicate the anti-toxicity of catalysts. Specific catalytic performance information was listed in Table 1 . The result demonstrates the Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20 catalyst has better anti-poisonous ability than that of rest catalysts. Besides, the peak potential of methanol oxidation decreased from −0.207 V (Au@N-CQDs@Pd 10) to −0.217 V (Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20). The negative shift of the anode potentials of methanol oxidation manifests the smaller overpotential and higher electrocatalytic activity of the catalysts.

From the above results, it can be seen that the synthetic catalyst has the best catalytic activity when the preparation temperature is 20 °C. In order to explore the role of the introduction of N-CQDs in MOR, the CVs of AuPd nanoparticles without N-CQDs and commercial Pd/C catalysts-modied electrodes were recorded under identical conditions for comparison. As shown in Fig. 7A, the ECSA values of Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20, AuPd and Pd/C were estimated to be 90.54 m2 g−1, 10.42 m2 g−1 and 5.18 m2 g−1, respectively. The current density of Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20 (25.08 mA cm−2) was approximately 2.5 and 5.9 times higher than that of AuPd (10.48 mA cm−2) and Pd/C (4.23 mA cm−2). Apart from that, the value of If/Ib for Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20 can be as high as 6.19. This value is much higher than that on AuPd (2.96) and Pd/C (4.12) in this work. Additionally, relevant studies have confirmed that the onset potential is also an important parameter for evaluating the low-potential catalytic performance of methanol oxidation [53]. For Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20, the onset potential is at about −0.52 V, which is more negative than −0.45 V of AuPd and − 0.38 V of Pd/C toward methanol oxidation in alkaline media. These results suggest the Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20 has remarkable electrocatalytic property and CO-poisoning tolerance, which mainly thanks to the addition of N-CQDs to enhance the overall tolerance of the material to CO. The electrochemical performances of the catalysts prepared in this work and some similar Pd-based electrocatalysts previously reported in the literature are listed in Table 2 . Apparently, as observed, Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20 also possesses better catalytic activity than these electrocatalysts. The outstanding electrochemical performance of Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20 is largely due to the core-shell dendritic structure providing more active sites, larger surface area, the interaction between the bimetal can prevent the accumulation of poisoned intermediates and more Pd site contacts with reactants [51,54]. In addition, the existence of N-CQDs can provide a fast pathway for the activation of water, thus speeding up the conversion rate of chemisorbed CO to CO2 [55]. The improvement of the electrochemical activity is inseparable from N-CQDs.

In order to research into the catalytic performance of Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20, experiments with different methanol concentrations and scanning rates were carried out. Fig. 8A shows the CV curves of Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20 in 1 M KOH at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 with diverse methanol concentrations (0.1 M - 4 M). As indicates from Fig. 8B, the peak current density of MOR increases with the increase of methanol concentration. Moreover, the peak potentials of methanol oxidation move in a positive direction as the methanol concentrations increase. The result shows that MOR is controlled by the methanol concentration [56]. Fig. 9A shows the CV curves of Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20 in 1 M KOH containing 0.5 M CH3OH at different scan rates. The peak current density and oxidation potential both increase with the increase of scan rate. There is a linear relationship between the peak current density in forward CV scan and the square root of scan rate, as shown in Fig. 9B, revealing the methanol oxidation reaction of Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20 is controlled by methanol diffusion [57].

To study the kinetics of electrode processes and surface phenomenon in the electrochemical system, in this work we analyzed electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) for Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20, AuPd nanoparticles, Pd/C and compared with bare GCE. A semicircular part is in the high frequency region, which corresponds to the electron transfer-controlled process; the straight line part is in the low frequency range, which represents the diffusion-controlled process [58]. The diameter of semicircle shows the charge transfer resistance (Rct), the larger of semicircle diameter signifies the greater charge transfer resistance [59]. The Nyquist curves in Fig. 10 show Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20 has much low charge transfer resistance than AuPd and Pd/C, indicating the present of N-CQDs can significantly accelerate electron transfer and manifest improved electrocatalytic performance.

It is worth mentioning that the stability of the catalyst is also a vital demand for the practical application of fuel cell. The long-term stability of Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20, AuPd and Pd/C catalysts was investigated using chronoamperometry (CA) in 1 M KOH containing 0.5 M CH3OH performs 3000 s test. As shown in Fig. 11 , the whole test is fast followed by slow. The current densities decrease rapidly, due to the generation of poisonous intermediates on the catalyst surface during the oxidation process. The catalytic current density of Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20 slowly drops down but still retains a high current density even after 3000 s, whereas the AuPd and Pd/C catalysts show a rapid decay and display a lower current density closed to zero under the identical conditions.

These above results demonstrate the good resistence to the CO-like intermediates, improved electrocatalytic performance and good stability of the Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20 are attributed to the special structures and the synergistic effects between Au and Pd [50]. Overall, the results of electrocatalytic tests show that Au@N-CQDs@Pd 20 catalyst is an anode catalyst with great potential in alkaline methanol.

In summary, Au@N-CQDs@Pd electrocatalysts for MOR were successfully synthesized via a simple and green two-step method, using N-CQDs as the reducing agent, stabilizing agent and shape-directing agent. The morphology of catalyst can be affected by temperature. Au@N-CQDs@Pd obtained at 20 °C show the enhanced electrocatalytic activity and stability toward methanol oxidation, compared with the AuPd nanoparticles and Pd/C in alkaline medium. The role of N-CQDs can be described in following aspects: (1) N-CQDs have abundant oxygen-containing groups (such as -OH, -COOH) to become the nucleation center of Pd nanoparticles deposition and prevent the agglomeration, further forming a dendritic Pd shell, providing a larger specific surface area and exhibiting more active sites. (2) N-CQDs can also accelerate the chemisorbed conversion of CO to CO2. (3) As a novel carbon material support, N-CQDs greatly accelerate the electrode transfer between catalyst and methanol. Therefore, the above results improve the electrocatalytic activity, tolerance CO and stability of Au@N-CQDs@Pd for MOR.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial was sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21165016, 21175108, 21265018) and the Science and Technology Support Projects of Gansu Province (Nos. 1011GKCA025, 090GKCA036, 1208RJZM289) and the Innovation Capacity Improvement Plan of Northwest Normal University (CX2018Y007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2020.114423.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bimetallic Pt-au nanocatalysts electrochemically deposited on graphene and their electrocatalytic characteristics towards oxygen reduction and methanol oxidation
Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys.
